shortDescription.text: No validation or sanitization of filename parameter allows path traversal
Storage operations use caller-supplied filename directly without authorization checks
SAS token cached in Redis without encryption
SAS token generated with overly broad permissions
Download target path not validated, enabling arbitrary file write
Bare except clause silences all errors including security-relevant ones
Reading credentials from .env file at runtime may expose secrets

message.text: The 'filename' parameter in save/load_once/load_stream/download/delete methods across all storage backends is passed directly to cloud storage APIs (S3 Key, Azure blob name, GCS blob name, etc.) without any sanitization. An attacker who controls the filename can use sequences like '../' or absolute paths to read/write/delete arbitrary objects in the storage bucket. None of the 7 storage backends perform path normalization, prefix-bounding, or validation on the filename.

Azure Blob Storage save/load/download/delete methods pass the filename directly to Azure SDK blob operations without path sanitization. An attacker controlling the filename can traverse to arbitrary blobs in the container.
Google Cloud Storage operations use the filename directly as the blob name without validation. Path traversal characters in the filename can target arbitrary objects in the GCS bucket.

Aliyun OSS uses posixpath.join(self.folder, filename) which does NOT prevent path traversal. If filename is an absolute path (e.g. '/etc/passwd') or contains '../' sequences, posixpath.join will resolve to a path outside the intended folder prefix, allowing access to arbitrary objects in the bucket.
Tencent COS storage passes filename directly as the Key parameter without sanitization, allowing path traversal to arbitrary objects in the bucket.
Supabase storage passes filename directly to upload/download/delete methods without sanitization, allowing path traversal to arbitrary objects in the bucket.

OpenDAL storage passes filename directly to op.write/op.read/op.delete without sanitization. When scheme is 'fs' (local filesystem), this is especially dangerous as it enables reading/writing arbitrary files on the host filesystem.

The BaseStorage interface and all 7 implementations accept a raw filename string without any authorization context (user ID, tenant ID, session). Any caller that controls the filename can access any file in the bucket. The storage layer has no concept of ownership or access control — it relies entirely on the caller to construct safe filenames. If any upstream code path passes user-controlled input as the filename, it results in insecure direct object reference.

Azure Blob Storage caches the generated SAS token in Redis using a predictable key pattern 'azure_blob_sas_token_{account_name}_{account_key}'. The SAS token is stored as plaintext in Redis. Additionally, the cache key itself contains the account_key, which means the secret key is used as part of a Redis key visible to anyone with Redis access. If Redis is compromised, both the SAS token and the account key pattern are exposed.

The Azure SAS token is generated with very broad permissions: read, write, delete, list, add, create on all resource types (service, container, object). This violates the principle of least privilege. A leaked SAS token would grant full control over the storage account. The token should be scoped to only the specific container and operations needed.

The download() method in multiple backends (S3, Azure, GCS, Supabase, OpenDAL) writes to target_filepath without any validation. If an attacker controls target_filepath, they can write downloaded content to arbitrary locations on the filesystem. The Azure implementation uses open() directly; Supabase uses Path.write_bytes(); OpenDAL uses Path.write_bytes(). None validate that the target path is within an expected directory.

The exists() method in AwsS3Storage uses a bare 'except:' clause that catches all exceptions including KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit. This silently swallows errors that could indicate security issues such as permission denied (403), which would incorrectly return False instead of raising an error. A 403 response might indicate a misconfigured bucket policy that should be investigated.

OpenDAL storage reads credentials from a .env file at runtime using dotenv_values(). This means sensitive cloud storage credentials (access keys, secret keys) may be stored in a .env file on disk. If the application is compromised via another vulnerability (e.g., the path traversal in this same storage layer), the .env file could be read to exfiltrate all cloud credentials.
